###### 13.1.2 Regression Assumptions and Sampling Variation

THINKING ABOUT WHATʼS IN THE ERROR TERMleads us to the firstassumptionweʼll need to make about the error term. There are going to be a few of these assumptions, and Iʼll bring them up as they become necessary. People who use regression tend to be obsessed about those lilʼ s and the assumptions we make about them. Go to any research seminar, and youʼll hear endless questions about error terms and whether the assumptions we need to make about them are likely to be true.

ε

The first assumption weʼll talk about links the first and second parts of the book, and itʼs the exogeneityassumption. If we want to say that our OLS estimates of will, on average, give us

β1

β1 X ε 4

the population , then it must be the case that isuncorrelatedwith .

If that sounds familiar, itʼs because weʼre really just restating the conditions for identifying a causal effect! Every variable that causes in a causal diagram like Figure13.213.2 ends up in the13.2.... regression equation somehow, whether itʼs one of the variables in the model or just in . If a variable isin the regression equation directly, then that closes any causal paths that go through that variable. By adding a variable to the regression, we “control for it” or “add it as a control.” So with the regression , the path is closed.

###### Y

ε

Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + ε X ← Z → Y

But if something is still in the error term and is correlated with , we havenʼt closed the path. Since is in , and we know from the diagram that , then we can say either that (a) we havenʼt closed the back door path, and so the effect of isnʼt identified, or, (b) is correlated with and so is “endogenous” rather than exogenous, and the effect of isnʼt identified. These are saying, in effect, the same thing, just using lingo from different domains.

X A ε A → X

X ← A → Y X X ε X

5 6

Similarly, we can say in either set of lingo that we donʼt need to worry about being in our model. Itʼs in the error term - everything that predicts but isnʼt in the model is in the error term. But we can say either (a) thereʼs no back door from to that goes through , or (b) is unrelated to and so isnʼt leading to be endogenous.

C Y

X Y C X C C X

Speaking of lingo, if wedohave that endogeneity problem, then on average our estimate of wonʼtgive us the population value. When that happens - when our estimate on average gives us the wrong answer, we call that abiasin our estimate. In particular, this form of bias, where we are biased because a variable correlated with is in the error term, is known asomited variable bias,since it happens because we omitted an important variable from the regression equation. Previously in the book weʼd say we failed to close the back door that goes through . In this lingo, weʼd say that gave us omitted variable bias, but doesnʼt.

β1

X

A A C

So thatʼs our first assumption about the error term - the exogeneity assumption, that is uncorrelated with any variable we want to know the causal effect of. We know how to think about whether that assumption is true - the entire first half of this book is about figuring out whatʼs necessary to identify a causal effect.

ε

Of course, thatʼs only the first thing we need to assume about the error term. Many of the others, though, relate to the sampling variation of the OLS estimates.

JUST LIKE THE MEANS WE DISCUSSEDin Chapter33, regression coefficients are estimates, and even3 though thereʼs a true population model out there, the estimate we get varies from sample to sample due to sampling variation.

Conveniently, we have a good idea what that sampling variation looks like. We know that we can think of observations as being pulled from theoretical distributions. We also know that statistics like the mean can also be thought of as being pulled from theoretical distributions. In the case of the mean, thatʼs a normal distribution (see Chapter33). If you drew a whole bunch of samples of3 the population and took the mean each time, the distribution of means across the sample would follow a normal distribution. Then we can use the estimated mean we get to try to figure out what the population mean is.

Regression coefficients also follow a normal distribution, and we know what the mean and standard deviation of that normal distribution is. Or, at least we do if we make a few more assumptions about the error term.

7

What is that normal distribution that the OLS coefficients follow?

In a regression model with one predictor, like , an OLS estimate of the truemodel population follows a normal distribution with a mean of and a standard deviation of

Y = β0 + β1X + ε β^1 β1 β1

, where is the number of observations in the data, is the standard deviation of the error term , and is the variance of . This standard deviation isnʼt generally caleda standard deviation, though. The standard deviation of a sampling distribution is often referred to as astandard error. And thatʼs what weʼll call them here too.

√σ2/(var(X)n) n σ ε var(X) X 8

If thereʼs more than one variable in the model, the math starts to require matrix algebra, but itʼs the same idea. In the regression model , the OLS estimates and follow a joint normal distribution, where their respective means are the population and , and the standard deviations are the square roots of the diagonal of , where is a two-column matrix containing both and . But you can think of as saying “divide by

Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + ε β^1 β^2

β1 β2 σ2(A′A)−1/n A

X Z (A′A)−1

the variances and covariances of and ,” just like says “divide by the variance of .”

X Z √σ2/(var(X)n) X

How can we make an OLS estimateʼs sampling variation really small? There are only three terms in the standard deviation, so only three things to change: (1) We could shrink the standard deviation of the error term , i.e., make the model predict more accurately, (2) we could pick an

σ Y X X

that varies a lot and has a big variation - an that changes a lot makes it easier to check whether is changing at the same time, or (3) we could use a big sample so gets big.

Y n

HOW ABOUT THOSE OTHER ASSUMPTIONSwe need to make about the error term? It turns out that those assumptions are necessary to assume that the stuff Iʼve said up to now about the standard error is true. Letʼs tuck that away in our back pocket for now and ignore that looming problem. Weʼll come back to it in the “Your Standard Errors are Probably Wrong” section below. All of the things Iʼm going to say now aboutusingstandard errors will still hold up. There might just need to be some minor adjustments to the way we calculate them, which is what weʼll discuss in that section.

###### 13.1.3 Hypothesis Testing in OLS

OKAY, SO WHY DID WE WANT TO KNOW THE OLS COEFFICIENT DISTRIBUTION AGAIN?The same reason we want to think aboutanytheoretical distribution - theoretical distributions let us use what we observeto come to the conclusion that certain theoretical distributions are unlikely.

And since the theoretical distribution of our OLS estimate is centered around the population parameter , that means we can use our estimate to say that certain population parameters are very unlikely, for example “itʼs unlikely that the effect of on is 13.2.”

β^1 β1 β^1

X Y

To recap what weʼre doing here, modifying only slightly what we have from Chapter33:3

- 1. We pick a theoretical distribution, specifically a normal distribution


centered around a particular value of

β1

- 2. Estimate using OLS in our observed data, getting
- 3. Use that theoretical distribution to see how unlikely it would be to get if indeed the true population value is what we picked in the first step
- 4. If itʼs super unlikely, that initial value we picked is probably wrong!


β1

β^1

β^1

9

We can take this a step further towards the concept ofhypothesis testing. Hypothesis testing is a way of formalizing the four steps above into a way of making a decision about whether we can “reject” the theoretical distribution, and thus the original we picked.

β1 10

Under hypothesis testing, we pick a “null hypothesis” - the initial value weʼre going to check against. In practice, this is almost always zero (although it certainly doesnʼt have to be), so letʼs simplify and say that our null hypothesis is .

β1

β1 = 0

Then, we pick a “rejection value” . If we calculate that the probability of getting our estimated result from the theoretical distribution based on is below , then we say thatʼs too unlikely, and conclude that is false, rejecting it. Commonly, this rejection value is , so if the estimate we get (or something even stranger) has a 5% chance or less of occurring if the null value is true, then we reject the null.

α

β1 = 0 α β1 = 0 α = .05

Once weʼve rejected , what conclusion can we come to? Not that our estimate is correct we certainly donʼt know that; there are plenty of nonzero values we havenʼt rejected. All we can say is that we donʼt think itʼs likely to be 0. Knowing itʼs not zero can be handy - if itʼs not zero, that means thereʼssomerelationship there. And thatʼs our conclusion.

β1 = 0

To see this in action, I generated 200 random observations using the true model , where is normally distributed with mean 0 and variance 1. Then, pretending that we donʼt know the truth is , I estimated the OLS model using the regression .

Y = 3 + .2X + ε ε

β1 = .2 Y = β0 + β1X + ε

The first estimate I get is . I can use the formula from the last section to also calculate that the standard error of is . So the theoretical distribution Iʼm looking at is a normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation .

β^1 = .142 β1 se(β1) = .077

###### .077

Under that distribution, the estimate I got is at the 96.7th percentile of the theoretical distribution, as shown in Figure13.313.3. That means that something as far from 0 as13.33.33.33333333.33.3333333 is (or farther) happens 6.6% of the time. If we started with , then we wouldnotreject the null hypothesis, since 6.6% is higher than 5%, even though we happen to know for a fact that the null is quite wrong.

###### .142

###### .142 (100 − 96.7) × 2 = 3.3 × 2 = 11 α = .05 β1 = 0

Figure 13.3: Comparison of Our .142 Estimate to a Theoretical Null Distribution Centered at 0

![image 76](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile76.png)

We can see how this works from a different angle in Figure13.413.4. This time Iʼm generating a bunch13.4.... of samples of random data and showing the sampling distribution against a given null value. Iʼm magically giving myself the knowledge that is the true value to test against. I generate

###### β1 = .2

random data from that same true model a whole bunch of times, estimate using OLS, and test it against the null of . Sometimes I still reject the null, even though the null is literally true, but not often. In fact, I reject it exactly 5% of the time, because of the decision. Sampling variation will do that! This is a rejection of something thatʼs true, and how often we do it is the “false positive” rate, or the terribly-named “type I error rate.”

Y = 3 + .2X + ε β^1 β1 = .2

α = .05

12

I then test those same estimates against the null of . Sometimes I fail to reject the null, even though the null is false. Sampling variation does this too! Our failure to reject false nulls is the “false negative” rate or the “type II error rate.”

###### β1 = 0

What these figures demonstrate is that hypothesis testing really isnʼt about pinning down a true value, nor is it about hard-and-fast, true-and-false results. Itʼs about showing that certain theoretical distributions are unlikely. And we can then say that if theyʼretoounlikely, we can forget about them.

![image 77](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile77.png)

Figure 13.4: Testing 1,000 Randomly Generated Samples Against the True Null and a False Null

In practice, when applied to regression in most contexts, weʼre talking about a normally-distributed regression coefficient and an

false positive rate (with a two-tailed test), testing against a null that the coefficient is 0. So if the probability of our estimate (or something even farther

###### α = .05

from 0) occurring is less than 5%, we call that “statistically significant” and say the relationship is nonzero.

The calculation of the probability comes from computing the percentiles of the normal distribution with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of our estimated . If our estimate is below the 2.5th percentile or above the 97.5th, thatʼs statistically significant with .

se(β^1) β^1 α = .05

We can also look at the exact percentile we get and evaluate that. If, for example, we find that our estimate of is at the 1.3rd percentile, then we can double that to 2.6, and say that we have a

β^1

- 2.6% probability of being that far away from the null or farther. This probability is called the “pvalue.” The lower that p-value is, the lower the chance is of finding a result that far from the null or farther by sampling variation alone. If you look back at Figure13.313.3, the entire shaded area is13.33.33.33333333.33.3333333 the p-value, since itʼs the area as far away from the null as the actual estimate is, or farther. If the p-value is below , thatʼs statistical significance.


α

Using some known properties of the normal distribution, we can take a shortcut without having to go to the trouble of calculating percentiles. In this context, thet-statisticis the coefficient divided by its standard error . When we scale the estimate by its standard error, that puts us on the “standard normal” theoretical distribution where the standard deviation is 1. Then, if that t-statistic is below -1.96 (the 2.5th percentile) or above 1.96 (the 97.5th percentile), thatʼs statistically significant at the 95% level, and a nonzero relationship.

β^1/se(β^1)

Using that sort of interpretation is what lets us readregression tables, which weʼll get to in a second. But before we do, one aside on this whole concept of statistical significance. Rather, a plea.

Having taught plenty of students in statistical methods, and, further, talked with plenty of people who have received teaching in statistical methods, thesingle greatest differencebetween what studentsare taughtand what studentslearnis about statistical significance. I think this is because significance is wily and tempting. Powerful, tempting, seemingly simple, but so easy to misuse, and so easy to let it take you over and make you do bad things. So please keep the following things in mind and repeat them as a mantra at every possible moment until they live within you:

13

An estimatenotbeing statistically significant doesnʼt mean itʼs wrong. It just means itʼs not statistically significant.

Never, ever, ever, ever,ever, ever, ever give in to the thought: “oh no, my results arenʼt significant, Iʼd better change the analysis to get something significant.” Bad. Iʼm pretty sure professors always say to never do this, but students somehow remember the opposite.

14

15

A lot of stuff goes into significance besides just “is the true relationship nonzero or not.” - sampling variation, of course, and also the sample size, the way youʼre doing your analysis, your choice of , and so on. A significance test isnʼt the last word on a result.

###### α

16

Statistical significanceonlyprovides information about whether a particular null value is unlikely. It doesnʼt say anything about whether the effect youʼve foundmaters. In other words, “statistically significant” isnʼt the same thing as “significant.” A result showing that your treatment improves IQ by .000000001 points is not ameaningfuleffect, whether itʼs statistically significant or not.

17

Keep in mind, generally: the point of significance testing is to think about not just the estimates themselves but also the precision of those estimates. If youʼve got a really cool result but the

standard errors are huge, thereʼs a good chance itʼs a fluke that could be consistent with lots of truerelationships. Thatʼs the kind of thinking significance testing is meant to encourage. But there are other ways to keep precision in mind. You could just think about the standard errors themselves; ask if the estimate is precise regardless of whether itʼs far from a given null value. You could ask what the range of reasonable null values is (construct a confidence interval) instead of focus on one in particular. You could go full Bayesian and do whatever the heck it is those crazy cats get up to. Significance testing is just one way of doing it, and it has its pros and cons like everything else.

###### 13.1.4 Regression Tables and Model-Fit Statistics

WE HAVE A DECENT IDEA AT THIS POINTof how to think about the line that an OLS estimation produces, as well as the statistical properties of its coefficients. But how can we interpret the model as a whole? How can we make sense of a whole estimated regression at once? For that we can turn to the most common way that regression results are presented: the regression table.

Weʼre going to run some regressions using data on restaurant and food inspections. We might be curious whether chain restaurants get better health inspections than restaurants with fewer (or only one) location. Weʼll be using this data throughout this chapter. Some basic summary statistics for the data are in Table13.111111113.1. We have data on the inspection score (with a maximum111113.1.1.1.1.1111111 score of 100), the year the inspection was performed, and the number of locations that restaurant chain has.

What can we see in Table13.213.2? Each column represents a different regression. The first column of13.2.... results shows the results from the Equation(13.2)(((13.2) regression, and the second column of results((13.2).... ))) shows the results from the Equation(13.3)(((13.3) regression.((13.3)3.3)3.3)3333333.33.3)333333

The first thing we notice is that each variable used to predict Inspection Score gets its own set of two rows. The “Intercept” also gets two rows - this is , and on some tables is called the “Constant.” The first row shows the coefficient estimate. Our estimate , the coefficient on Number of Locations, is . It happens to be the same in both regressions - the addition of Year as a control didnʼt change the estimate enough to notice. There are also some asterisks - Iʼll get to those in a second.

β^0

β^1 β^1 = −.0019

19

Below the coefficient estimates, we have our measures of precision, in parentheses. In particular, in this table these are the standard errors of the coefficients. in this regression is - seems pretty precise to me. There are a few different ways you can measure the precision of a coefficient estimate. Putting standard errors here is the most common, but sometimes youʼll see something like a confidence interval. In some fields, using a t-statistic (coefficient divided by the standard error) is more common than the standard error. Ideally a table will tell you which one theyʼre doing, but not always!

se(β^1)

###### .0004

20

Finally, we have those asterisks, called “significance stars.” These let you know at a glance whether the coefficient is statistically significantly different from a null-hypothesis value of 0. To be really precise, these arenʼt exactly significance tests. Instead, theyʼre a representation of the pvalue. The lower the p-value is, the more stars we get.

21

Which p-value cutoff each number of stars corresponds to changes from field to field but should be described in the table note as it is here. A standard in many of the social sciences, and what youʼll see in this book, is that * means that the p-value is below .1 (10%), ** means itʼs below .05 (5%), and *** means itʼs below .01 (1%). So if we see *** as we do in this table for the coefficient on Number of Locations, that would mean thatif we had decided that (or higher), we would reject the null of . If we see ** then weʼd find statistical significance at or higher. Basically, itʼs a measure ofwhich values youʼd find significance withfor this coefficient. These stars are a way of, at a glance, being able to tell which of the coefficients are statistically significantly different from 0, which all of these are.

22

###### α = .01 β1 = 0 α = .05 α

23

Moving down the table, we have a bunch of things that arenʼt coefficients or precision measures. Which of these exact statistics are present will vary from table to table, but in general these are either descriptions of the analysis being run, or measures of the quality of the model. For the

24

first, itʼs common to see any additional details about estimation listed down here (such as any standard error adjustments), and just about every table will tell you the number of Observations (or sometimes “N”) included in estimating the regression.

For the second, there are a billion different ways to measure the quality of the model. Probably two of the most common are included here - and Adjusted . These are measures of the share of the dependent variableʼs variance that is predicted by the model. in the first model is

R2 R2

R2

, telling us that 6.5% of the variation in Inspection Score is predicted by the Number of Locations. If we were to predict Inspection Score with Number of Locations and then subtract out our prediction, weʼd be left with a residual variable that has only 93.5% of the variance of the original. Adjusted is the same idea, except that it makes an adjustment for the number of variables youʼre using in the model, so it only counts the variance explainedabove and beyondwhat youʼd get by just adding a random variable to the model.

.065

###### (100 − .065 =) R2

25

Finally, we have the “F-statistic.” This is a statistic used to do a hypothesis test. Specifically, it uses a null thatal the coefficents in the model(except the intercept/constant)are al zero at once, and tests how unlikely your results are given that null. Itʼs pretty rare that this will be

insignificant for any halfway-decent model, and so I for one mostly ignore this statistic.

Missing from this particular table, but present in a number of standard regression-table output styles, is the residual standard error, sometimes also called the root mean squared error (RMSE). This is, simply, our estimate of the standard deviation of the error term based on what we see in the standard deviation of the residuals. We take our predicted Inspection Score values based on our OLS model and subtract them from the actual values to get a residual. Then, we calculate the standard deviation of that residual and make a slight adjustment for the “degrees of freedom” the number of observations in the data minus the number of coefficients in the model. The bigger this number is, the bigger the average errors in prediction for the model are.

What can we do with all of these model-quality measures? Take a quick look, but in general donʼt be too concerned about these. These are usually measures of how well your dependent variable ispredictedby your OLS model. But if youʼre reading this book, youʼre probably not that concerned with prediction. Youʼre interested in identifying causal effects, and in estimating particular coefficients wel, rather than predicting the dependent variable overall.

If your or adjusted values are low, or your residual standard error is high, what this tells you is that thereʼsa lot going on with your dependent variable other than what youʼve modeled. Is that a concern? Maybe. If you thought you wrote down a diagram that explained your dependent variable super well and covered all of the things that cause it, and you chose your model and which variables to control for based on that… and then you got a tiny anyway, then your initial assumption that you really understood the data generating process of your dependent variable might be wrong. But if you donʼt care about most of the causes of your dependent variable and are pretty sure youʼve included the variables in your model necessary to identify your treatment, then is of little importance.

R2 R2

R2

R2

Similar to statistical significance, I see values as another thing that students tend to fixate on. Gets their buns in a knot. But while it can be a nice diagnostic, itʼs definitely not something to fixate on. Certainly donʼt design your model around maximizing - build the right model for identifying the effect you want to identify and answer your research question, not the right model for predicting the dependent variable. Even if you are interested in prediction, has plenty of flaws in use for building predictive models too, although thatʼs another book.

R2

26

R2

R2

KNOWING WHERE ALL THE PIECES OF A REGRESSION AREon a regression table, how can we interpret the results? Letʼs take a look at the regression table again in Table13.313.3.13.33.33.33333333.33.3333333
