A key phrase to keep in mind when interpreting the results of an OLS regression is “a one-unit change in…” Regression coefficients are all about estimating a linear relationship between two variables, and reporting the results in terms of the slope, i.e., the relationship between a one-unit change in the predictor variable and the dependent variable.

27

If we want to get real precise about it, the interpretation of an OLS coefficient on a variable is “controlling for the other variables in the model, a one-unit change in is linearly associated with a -unit change in .” If we want to get even more precise, we can say “If two observations have the same values of the other variables in the model, but one has a value of that is one unit higher, the observation with the one unit higher will on average have a that is units higher.”

β1 X X

β1 Y

X X Y β1

Letʼs start with the first column of results, with only one variable, a on Number of Locations. Letʼs think briefly about our units here. Number of Locations is the number of locations in the restaurant chain, and the dependent variable, Inspection Score, is an inspector score on a scale that goes up to 100.

−.019

Since we have no other control variables in the model, that means “a one-unit increase in the number of locations a chain restaurant has is linearly associated with a -point decrease in inspector score, on a scale of 0-100.” Or, “comparing two restaurants, the one thatʼs a part of a chain with one more location than the other will on average have an inspection score lower.”

−.019

−.019

−.019

Notice that however Iʼm wording it, Iʼm careful to avoid saying “a one-unit increase in number of locations decreases inspector score by .” This would be implying that Iʼve estimated a causal effect. I should only use this language if, based on what we learned in the first part of the book, we think weʼve identified the causal effect of number of locations on inspector score.

−.019

How about that constant term 94.866? This is our prediction for the dependent variable when all the predictor variables are zero. Think about it - our conditional mean of the dependent variable is whatever the OLS model spits out when we plug the appropriate values in. If we plug in 0 for everything, it all drops out and all we have left is our constant. So for a restaurant with zero locations, weʼd predict an inspector score of 94.866. Of course, itʼs impossible to have a restaurant with zero locations, so this doesnʼt mean much.

28

Letʼs move to the second column. Weʼre introducing a control variable here, year of inspection. It doesnʼt seem to change the coefficient on number of locations, but it does change the interpretation.

Now we need to incorporate the fact that our interpretation of each coefficient is based on the idea that we are “controlling for” the other variables in the model. We can say this in a few ways.

29

We can say “controlling for year of inspection, a one-unit increase in the number of locations a chain restaurant has is linearly associated with a -point decrease in inspector score, on a scale of 0-100.” Instead of “controlling for” we could instead say “adjusting for” or “adjusting linearly for” or even “conditioning on.”

###### −.019

- 30

−.019

- 31


We can say “comparing two inspections in the same year, the one for a restaurant thatʼs a part of a chain with one more location than the other will on average have an inspection score lower.” After all, thatʼs the idea of controlling for variables. We want to compare like-with-like and close back doors, and so weʼre trying to remove the part of the Number of Locations/Inspector Score relationship that is driven by Year of Inspection. The idea is that by including a control for Year, we are removing the part explained by Year, and can proceed as though the remaining estimates are comparing two inspections that effectively have the same year. Thereʼs no variation in year left - we have held year constant. For this reason you will also often hear the interpretation “holding year of inspection constant, a one-unit increase in the number of locations is associated with a decrease in inspector score.”

###### −.019

This is a simple guide to interpreting a regression, and one thatʼs focused pretty heavily on semantics. But training yourself to explain regression in these terms will truly help you interpret

- them better. The same intuition will carry over when you start doing regression in more complex settings, as weʼll discuss throughout the chapter.


###### 13.1.5 Subscripts in Regression Equations

IʼVE LEFT OUT ONE COMMON FEATURE OF EXPRESSING A REGRESSION.When writing out the equation for a regression, people will commonly usesubscriptson their variables (i.e., ). We already have subscripts on our coefficients (the 1 in , etc.), but they show up on the variables themselves, too. Iʼve chosen mostly to omit these in this book, but itʼs important to be familiar with the concept when youʼre reading papers or writing your own.

Xlittletextdownhere β1

32

A regression might be expressed as

Yi = β0 + β1Xi + εi (13.4)

The here tells uswhat index the data varies across. In other words, a single observation is a whatexactly? Here we have , which generally means “individual,” i.e., an individual person or firm or country, depending on context. In this regression, and differ across individuals.

i

i

###### Y X

Alternately, we might see

Yt = β0 + β1Xt−1 + εt (13.5)

The here would be shorthand for time period. This is describing a regression where each observation is a different time period. The tells us that we are relating from a given period

t

Xt−1 Y t X (t − 1)

to the from the period before .

The subscripts do their best work when there are multiple axes that thingscouldvary along. Consider this example:

Yit = βg + βt + β1Xit + β2Wi + εit (13.6)

This is describing a regression in which and vary across a set of individuals and across time

Y X i t βt

(a panel data set). There is a different intercept for each time period , and also a different intercept for each value of (and whatʼs ? Weʼd have to look for where the researcher is describing their regression, but it might mean some rouping that the individuals are sorted into). We also see a control variable . The subscript here (and lack of a time subscript ) tells us that only varies across individual and doesnʼt change over time. Perhaps is something like birthplace that is different for individuals but does not vary over time.

g g

g i

Wi i t W W

###### 13.1.6 Turning a Causal Diagram into a Regression

OKAY, SO WEʼVE COVERED THE BASICS OF HOW REGRESSION WORKS.And we spent the first half of this whole book talking about how to identify causal effects using causal diagrams.

Itʼs tempting to think that we automatically know how to link the two, but Iʼve found that students often have difficulty making this jump. Itʼs not asuper difficultjump, mind you, but itʼs not one we can take for granted.

The chart in Figure13.513.5 may help. On the diagram, weʼre thinking about what to do with a13.5.... variable . Is it the outcome variable, or is some other variable the outcome? Is it the treatment, or is some other variable the treatment? If itʼs neither, where does it go on the diagram and also in a regression, if anywhere at all?

###### A Y X

![image 78](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile78.png)

![image 79](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile79.png)

4/18/26, 1:52 PM Chapter 13 - Regression | The Effect

As shown in Figure13.513.5, each variable may play one of several13.5.... roles in a regression. It could be our outcome variable - we know all about those. Outcome variables become the dependent variables in regression, the part of . We know

Figure 13.5: Chart for Constructing a Regression Equation

Y = Y = β0 + β1X

all about treatment variables from our discussion of causal diagrams, too. Those become the part.

β1X

How about the rest? Our causal diagram tells us all about variables that need to be adjusted for in order to close back doors (and also those thatshouldnʼtbe controlled for to avoid closing front doors or opening back doors by controlling for colliders). Anything that should be included as a control should be, well, included as a control. Thatʼs the part of , where is the treatment and is the outcome.

+β2A Y = β0 + β1X + β2A X Y

There are two things we havenʼt come to yet that show up in regression but not so much in causal diagrams. First, what if a variable doesnʼtneed to becontrolled for, but also controlling for it wonʼt break the identification? In that case wecaninclude it as a control, and maybe we want to as long as is related to , then adding it will explain more of the variation in , reducing variance in the error term. And since the standard errors of the regression coefficients like rely on the variance of the error term, the standard errors will shrink.

A

A Y Y

β^1

Finally, what about that part of the graph where it asks about whether the effect of treatment will differ across values of ? Thatʼs whereinteraction termscome in. Weʼll talk about those later in the chapter.

A

###### 13.1.7 Coding Up a Regression

BEFORE WE MOVE ON, WE SHOULD PROBABLY ACTUALLY DO A REGRESSION.Seems like a good idea.

The following code chunks will replicate Tables13.213.2 and13.2.... 13.313.3 in R, Stata, and Python. They will13.33.33.33333333.33.3333333 load in the restaurant inspection data, calculate the number of locations, regress inspection score on the number of locations, do another regression that also includes year as a control, and

- then output a regression table to file.


Note that while the code for performing a regression is fairly standard in each language, the process for producing a regression table generally depends on downloadable packages. There are plenty of options for these packages. Iʼll be using the modelsummary R package (install.packages('modelsummary')), estout for Stata (ssc install estout), and stargazer for Python when thereʼs more than one regression to display (pip install stargazer). But some good alternatives include export_summs from the jtools package, or outreg2, regsave, or the Stata 17 exclusive collect in Stata.33

###### R Code R Code e Stata CodeStata CodeStatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatata e Python CodePython Codeytyttt ooooooooooo o o oooooo o o o e

library(tidyverse); library(modelsummary) res <- causaldata::restaurant_inspections

res <- res %>% # Create NumberofLocations group_by(business_name) %>% mutate(NumberofLocations = n())

# Perform the first, one-predictor regression # use the lm() function, with ~ telling us what # the dependent variable varies over m1 <- lm(inspection_score ~ NumberofLocations, data = res)

# Now add year as a control # Just use + to add more terms to the regression m2 <- lm(inspection_score ~ NumberofLocations + Year, data = res)

# Give msummary a list() of the models we want in our table # and save to the file "regression_table.html" # (see help(msummary) for other options) msummary(list(m1, m2),

stars=TRUE, output= 'regression_table.html')

# Default significance stars are +/*/**/*** .1/.05/.01/.001. Social science # standard */**/*** .1/.05/.01 can be restored with msummary(list(m1, m2),

stars=c('*' = .1, '**' = .05, '***' = .01), output= 'regression_table.html')

###### 13.2 Getting Fancier with Regression

REGRESSION IS A TOOL- averyflexible tool. And weʼve only really learned one way to use it! This section isnʼt even going to change how we use it; itʼs just going to change the variables that go into it. This in itself will open up a whole new world.

So far, weʼve talked about regression of the format . Weʼre still working with that. But weʼve also always been working withcontinuousvariables included in the regression as their normal selves. Is there something else?

Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + ε

First, we might not have continuous variables. Instead, we might have discrete variables, which most often pop up asbinaryvariables - true or false, rather than a particular number. How can we interpret if is “this person has blonde hair”? Or, how would we control for “hair color” if thatʼs not a variable weʼve measured continuously, and we instead have categories like “black,” “brown,” “blonde,” and “red”?

β2 Z

Second, we might not use variables as they are but firsttransformthem. We talked way back in Chapter44 that some relationships might not be well-described by straight lines. Sometimes we4 want curvy lines. How can we do that, exactly?

Third, therelationships themselvesmight be affected by other variables. For example, back in Chapter44 we talked about a study by Emily Oster that asked “is the relationship between taking4 vitamin E and health outcomes stronger during the period when vitamin E was recommended by doctors than during the periods when it wasnʼt?” We can model the relationship between taking vitamin E and health outcomes with . But how can we model howthat relationship changesover time? We need aninteraction.

Outcomes = β0 + β1V itaminE + ε

So those three things - all of which have to do with the kinds of variables we use as predictors in our OLS model - will be where we start. Once weʼve covered that, weʼll move on to how we deal with standard errors, and when ourdependent variableisnʼt continuous either.

###### 13.2.1 Handling Discrete Variables

BINARY VARIABLES ARE ABSOLUTELY EVERYWHERE IN SOCIAL SCIENCE.Did you get the treatment or not? Are you left-handed or right-handed? Are you a man or a woman? Are you Catholic or not? Are you married or not?

34

Binary variables are especially important for causal analysis, since a lot of the causes we tend to be interested in are binary in nature. Did you get the treatment or not?

Any time we have something that youareorare not, which happens any time we have some sort of qualitative description, we are dealing with a binary variable. These can be included in regression models just as normal. So we can still be working with , but now maybe or (or both) can only take two values: 0 (“are not”/false) or 1 (“are”/true). If the binary variable is a control variable, we can think of it as we normally do - weʼre just shutting off back doors that go through the variable. But what if we are interested in the effect of that binary variable? How can we interpret the binary variableʼs coefficient?

###### Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + ε X Z 35

36

Simply put, itʼs thedifferencein the dependent variable between the trues and the falses. So for example, if we ran the regression , where is a binary variable thatʼs 1 whenever itʼs Winter and 0 when itʼs not, then would behow much higher sales are on average in Winter than in Not Winter.

###### Sales = β0 + β1Winter + ε Winter β1

37

When we estimate the model, if we got , then weʼd say that, on average, sales are 5 lower in Winter than they are in Not Winter. Take a look at the simulated data in Table13.413.4. Notice that13.4.... the coefficient on Winter (-5) is just the difference between the mean for Not Winter (15) and the mean for winter (10). Also notice that the coefficient on the intercept (15) is the expected mean when all the variables are zero - when Winter is 0, weʼre in Not Winter, and average sales in Not Winter are 15.

###### β^1 = −5

Table 13.4: Average Sales by Season, or Regression of Sales on Winter

Mean OLS Estimates Winter 10.000 -5.000

(1.103) Not Winter 15.000 (Ref.) (Intercept) 15.000

(3.413)

Note: Standard errors are in parentheses. No significance stars are shown.

One important thing to note is that weonly include one sideof the yes/no question. The model is

, not . Why is this? First off, imagine trying to interpret that. We want to compare Winter to Not Winter, but

###### Sales = β0 + β1Winter + ε Sales = β0 + β1Winter + β2NotWinter + ε β1 β2

compares Not Winter to Winter. So… whatʼs the difference between them? Interpretation would be pretty confusing. Second, this is to satisfy another OLS assumption we have to make, that there is noperfect multicolinearity. That is, you canʼt make a perfect linear prediction of any of the variables in the model with any of the other variables. Here, . We donʼt have a 1 in our model, though, so no problem, right? Wrong! There is an all-1s variable lurking in our model at all times—itʼs being multiplied by the constant!

Winter + NotWinter = 1

Why canʼt we have that linear combination? Because OLS wouldnʼt be able to figure out how to estimate the parameters. Imagine the average sales in Winter are 10, and in Not Winter itʼs 15. If your regression is , you could generate those exact same predictions with . Or with . Or with

Sales = β0 + β1Winter + β2NotWinter + ε

β0 = 15,β1 = −5,β2 = 0 β0 = 10,β1 = 0,β2 = 5 β0 = 3,β1 = 7,β2 = 12

. Or any infinite number of other ways! And while theyʼd allwork, OLS has no way of picking one estimate out of those many, many options. It canʼt give you a best estimate any more. We need to limit its options by dropping NotWinter, in effect forcing it to choose the

version.

β0 = 15,β1 = −5,β2 = 0

WE CAN EXPAND OUR INTUITION ABOUT BINARY VARIABLES JUST A BITand give ourselves the ability to includecategoricalvariables in our model. Binary variables are just yes/no. But categorical variables can take any number of categories. These include variables like “what country do you live in?” or “what decade were you born in?” You canʼt answer these questions with yes and no, but theyarediscrete and mutually exclusive categories.

38

We can handle categorical variables by justgiving each of the categories its own binary variable. So instead of one variable for “which country do you live in?” itʼs one variable for “do you live in France?”, another for “do you live in Gambia?”, another for “do you live in New Zealand?” and so on. If weʼre including the categorical variable as a control, by including these binary variables for each category we can say that weʼve closed the back doors that go through, in this example, which country you live in.

###### Income = β0 + β1France + β2Gambia + β3NewZealand+...

And what if we want to interpret the coefficient on one of these binary categories? This is just a hair trickier than with a binary variable. Just like we couldnʼt include both sides of the binary variable in the model (we had to put just Winter in the model, not Winter and NotWinter), we also

canʼt include every single category in the model. We need to drop one of them. The one we drop then becomes the “reference category.” All the coefficients arerelative to that category.

Before, with binary variables, the coefficient gave the difference between “yes” and “no.” Now, with a categorical variable and a reference category, itʼs the difference between “this category” and “the reference category.”

39

For example, if we drop France from our regression, France becomes the reference category. If we then estimate and get and , then that meansaverage income in Gambia is .5 higher than average income in Franceand average income in New Zealand is 3 higher than average income in France.Both of the interpretations only take things relative to the reference category, not each other.

Income = β0 + β1Gambia + β2NewZealand+... β^1 = .5 β^2 = 3

We can see this in the simulated data in Table13.513.5, which includes data only for France, Gambia,13.5.... and New Zealand. Notice that average income for Gambia (30.5) is higher than the average income for France (30), and the coefficient on Gambia when France is the reference category is . Similarly, the average income for New Zealand (33) is 3 higher than France, and the coefficient is

###### .5

###### .5

3. The intercept is the average income when all the predictors are 0 - so itʼs not Gambia, and itʼs not New Zealand, meaning it must be France. The average income in France is 30, so the intercept is 30.

Table 13.5: Average Income by Country among France, Gambia, and New Zealand, or Regression of Income on Country

Mean OLS Estimates France 30.000 (Ref.) Gambia 30.500 .500

(.103) New Zealand 33.000 3.000

(1.321) (Intercept) 30.000

(5.412)

Note: Standard errors are in parentheses. No significance stars are shown.

This reference category stuff means that the coefficients on the categories (and their significance) have relatively little meaning on their own. They only have meaning relative to each other. The coefficients change completely if you change the reference category. If we made New Zealand the reference category instead, the coefficient on Gambia would change from .5 to -2.5.

40

If you want to know whether a categorical variable has a significant effect as a whole, you donʼt look at the individual coefficients. Instead, you look atalthe category coefficients. This takes the form of a “joint F test,” where you compare the predictive power of the model against a version where the categorical variable has been removed as a predictor.

In R, the linearHypothesis command in the car package can perform the joint F test. You give it the full list of names of coefficients for the categories (which you can make yourself using paste and unique with a little effort, or the matchCoefs() function). Slightly easier to use is wald() in the fixest package, which lets you easily pattern-match variable names, although this also requires the use of fixestʼs feols() regression function instead of lm(). In Stata, testparm i.cat will do a joint significance test of all the categories of the cat variable. In Python, if you use smf.OLS().fit() to create a regression results object, letʼs say itʼs called m1, then you can do a joint F test with m1.f_test(). Youʼll need to read the documentation to see how to create a matrix or string indicating the factor variables to be tested.

###### 13.2.2 Polynomials

SOMETIMES, A STRAIGHT LINE ISNʼT ENOUGH.OLS assumes that the relationship between the dependent variable and each of the predictor variables can be described as a straight line (linear). If thatʼs not true, OLS will do a poor job. For example, look at Figure13.613.6. The true13.6.... relationship clearly follows the curvy line drawn on the right. If we try to estimate this relationship using a straight line, we get something that doesnʼt fit very well, shown by the line on the left.

###### Figure 13.6: Math Scores and Intelligence: OLS and the True Model

![image 80](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile80.png)

We can still use OLS for these kinds of relationships though. We just need to be careful to model that curviness right in our regression equation.

There are two main ways we can do this: we can eitheradd polynomial terms, or we can transform the data.

A POLYNOMIALis when you have the same variable in an equation as itself, as well as powers of itself. So for example, would be a “third-order polynomial” since it contains

###### β1X + β2X2 + β3X3 X X X2 X3 41,42

to the first power ( ), the second power ( ), and the third power ( ).

By adding these polynomial terms, itʼs possible to fit lines that arenʼt straight. In fact, add enough terms and you can mimic just about any shape. This can be seen in Figure13.713.7. Looking at the13.7.... data points directly, this is clearly a nonlinear relationship. On top of the data points we have the best-fit line from a linear model ( ), which gives the straight line, a second-order polynomial model, which gives the curvy dashed line ( ), and a third-order polynomial, which gives the curvy solid line ( ).

43

Y = β0 + β1X

Y = β0 + β1X + β2X2 Y = β0 + β1X + β2X2 + β3X3

Figure 13.7: Regression Using a Linear, Square, and Cubic Model

![image 81](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile81.png)

The curvy lines clearly do a better job fitting the relationship, and all we had to do was add those polynomial terms!

Thatʼs the reason to add polynomial terms then. Add polynomial terms and you can better fit a line to a non-straight relationship. But this leaves us with two pressing questions: (1) how can we interpret the regression model once we have polynomial terms, and (2) why not just add a bunch of polynomial terms all the time?

HOW CAN WE INTERPRET A MODEL WITH POLYNOMIALS?For this one, weʼre going to need a little calculus, Iʼm afraid. Letʼs take our cubic model from before:

Y = β0 + β1X + β2X2 + β3X3 (13.7)

Our typical interpretation of a regression coefficient estimate like would be “holding everything else constant, a one-unit increase in is associated with a -unit increase in .”

β^1 X β^1 Y

###### However, thatʼs a problem! Wecanʼt“hold everything else constant” because thereʼs no way to change without also changing and . So an interpretation of the effect of must take into account the coefficients oneachof its polynomial terms.When a regression model includes a polynomial for , the individual coefficients on the terms mean very litle on their own, and must be interpreted together.

X X2 X3 X

X X

How can we interpret them together? Letʼs try to get back our interpretation of “holding everything else constant, a one-unit increase in is associated with a (???)-unit increase in .” What is (???)? Well, what tool can we always reach for to figure out how one variable changes with another? The derivative! If we take the derivative of with respect to , that will tell us how a one-unit change in is related to .

X Y

Y X X Y 44

∂Y ∂X

= β1 + 2β2X + 3β3X2 (13.8)

X (β1 + 2β2X + 3β3X2) Y 45

A one-unit change in is associated with a -unit change in . Notice that the relationshipvaries with different values of . If , then the effect is . But if , then itʼs . The effect of on depends on the value of we already have. This is what weʼd expect. Look back at Figure13.713.7 - as13.7.... increases, at first increases as well, but then stops increasing and starts declining. The effect of a one-unit change in shouldvary as we move along the -axis.

X X = 1 β1 + 2β2 + 3β3 X = 2 β1 + 4β2 + 12β3 X Y X

X Y Y

X X

Letʼs use a more concrete example. Letʼs go back to our regression from Table13.213.2 but add a13.2.... squared term, which we can see in Table13.613.6.13.6....

Table 13.6: A Regression with a Second-Order Polynomial using Restaurant Inspection Data

&nbsp;Inspection Score Constant 97.5179***

(0.0588) Number of Locations −0.0802*** (0.0010) Number of Locations Squared 0.0001*** (0.0000)

* p < 0.1, ** p < 0.05, *** p < 0.01

&nbsp;Inspection Score Num.Obs. 27178 R2 0.194 R2 Adj. 0.194 F 3279.008 RMSE 5.62

* p < 0.1, ** p < 0.05, *** p < 0.01

Here we see a coefficient of on the linear term, and on the squared term. This means that a one-location increase in the number of locations associated with a restaurant chain is associated with a , or , change in the health inspection score. So for a chain with only one location, adding a second one would be associated with a reduction in the health score. But for a chain with 1000 locations, adding its 1001st would be associated with a

−.0802 .0001

β1 + 2β2NumberofLocations −.0802 + .0002NumberofLocations

−.0802 + .0002(1) = −.0800

increasein its health score. There is no single effect; it depends on what part of the -axis weʼre on. Leaving out the squared term would ignore that.

###### −.0802 + .0002(1000) = .1198 X

POLYNOMIALS SEEM PRETTY HANDY!So why not just add a whole bunch of them all the time? And while weʼre at it, why stop at just square terms and cubics? Why not add four-, five-, six-, or sevenorder polynomials to all our models?

There are a few good reasons. For one, the model does get a little harder to interpret. Of course, if itʼs the right model weʼd still want that anyway.

For another, often the higher-order polynomial terms donʼt really do anything. Take Figure13.713.713.7.... for example. The line fits from the second-order polynomial and the third-order polynomial models are almost exactly the same. So in many cases, by adding more and more polynomial terms, you make your model more complex without actually improving its fit.

46

There is also the issue that adding polynomial terms puts strain on the data and can lead to “overfitting,” where a too-flexible model winds up bending in strange shapes to try to fit noise in the data, producing a worse model. Imagine, for example, fitting a 100-order polynomial on a data set with 100 observations in it. Weʼd predict every point perfectly, but clearly that line isnʼt really going to tell us much of anything.

Adding more polynomial terms can, in general, make the model very sensitive to little changes in the data and can make its predictions kind of strange near the edges of the observed data. Take Figure13.813.8, for example, which starts with Figure13.8.... 13.713.7 and adds a regression fit using a ten-13.7.... degree polynomial. The shape doesnʼt seem to follow the data all that much better than even the two-order polynomial. And we get strange up-and-down motion that seems to chase individual observations rather than the overall relationship. The worst offender is over on the far right, where at the last moment the line springs way up to try to fit the single point on the far right of the data. Those additional degrees in the polynomial arenʼt just unnecessary, theyʼre actively making the model worse.

Figure 13.8: Regression Using a Linear, Square, Cubic, and Ten-degree Polynomial Model

![image 82](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile82.png)

So we donʼt want “too many” polynomial terms. But how much is just right? Well, ideally, you want to have the number of polynomial terms necessary to model the actual true underlying shape of the relationship. But we donʼt actually know what that is, so that doesnʼt help much.

A good way to approach the question of how many polynomial terms to include (or whether to include them at all) is graphically. This works best when you donʼt havetoomany observations. Just draw yourself a scatterplot and see what the shape of the data looks like. Then, add your regression line and see whether it seems to explain the shape you see. If it doesnʼt, try adding another polynomial term until it does.

You can also graphically analyze the residuals. Try a regression model, calculate the residuals, and then plotthoseon the -axis against your -axis variable. If you have enough polynomial terms, there shouldnʼt be any sort of obvious relationship between and the residuals. This can be seen in Figure13.913.9. The same data from Figure13.9.... 13.813.8 is estimated using a linear OLS and a13.8.... second-order polynomial OLS, and the residuals from those regressions are plotted against . After the linear regression, thereʼs still a clear shape to the relationship between the residuals and

y x

X

X

on the left. Not enough curviness to our line. But on the right, after our second-order polynomial, itʼs just a bunch of noise around 0. Thatʼs enough, we can stop!

- X


![image 83](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile83.png)

Figure 13.9: Residuals from Linear OLS and Second-Order Polynomial OLS

Besides all that, just as a good rule of thumb, you almost never want to go beyond cubic unless you have a really good reason. At

that point you have to start worrying that the statistical issues with too many terms are going to be a problem. The cure of polynomials may be worse than the disease of nonlinearity.

There are also more automatic approaches to selecting the number of polynomials. You donʼt have to look far to find people recommending that you try different amounts of polynomials, and then omit higher-order terms that are insignificant. In other words, try a quartic, and if the coefficient is insignificant, drop it. Then try the cubic, and if the term is insignificant… and so on. I donʼt recommend this approach, as you are at this point making decisions about your model based on a statistical significance test, and thatʼs not what statistical significance tests are meant to do.

###### X4 X3

47

A somewhat better approach is to use a principled model-selection algorithm like a Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO). Information on the specifics of LASSO will have to wait until the “Penalized Regression” section later in the chapter. For now, you can just think of it as a variant of OLS that tries to not just minimize the sum of squared errors, but it tries to do so while reducing the coefficients in the model. Like dropping polynomial terms because theyʼre insignificant, it takes the approach that additional polynomial terms that donʼt improve prediction much can be dropped. However, it does so in a way that is actually designed for the purpose of figuring out whether an additional term is “worth it,” rather than using significance testing, which only by happenstance sort of tries for a similar goal.

###### 13.2.3 Variable Transformations

POLYNOMIAL TERMS ARE NOT THE ONLY WAY TO FIT A NONLINEAR SHAPE.In many cases it makes more sense totransform the variable itselfdirectly.

Generally this means running a variable through a function before you use it. So for example, instead of running the regression

Y = β0 + β1X + ε (13.9)

you might instead run

Y = β0 + β1 ln(X) + ε (13.10)

###### ln() e 48

where is the base- logarithm, or the “natural log.”

Why might we do this? There are two main reasons we might want to transform a variable before using it, some of which we already covered in Chapter33.3

49

THE FIRST REASON MIGHT BEto give the variable statistical properties that play more nicely with regression. For example, you may want to reduceskew. A variable is “right skewed” if there are a whole lot of observations with low values and just a few observations withrealy highvalues. Income is a good example of a skewed variable. Most people are somewhere near the median income, but a few super-high earners like CEOs and movie stars earnway way waymore than the median.

50

- 51
- 52


Skewed data are likely to produceoutlierobservations. An outlier is an observation that has values that are very different from the other observations. For example, if you have a hundred people who all earn roughly $40,000 per year, and one person who earns a million, that person is an outlier.

Thereʼs nothing inherently wrong with outlier observations. Sometimes people take big outliers as indications that the data has not been recorded properly and that million-dollar-salary is more likely a hundred-thousand with a typo, but often the data is fine, itʼs just an outlier. However, even though the data may be accurate, the presence of outliers can make regression models statistically weak. Because the observations are way out there, they tend to produce big errors, and so just a few observations can have the effect of really tugging on the regression line and having way too much influence.

Using a transformation that “scrunches down” the big observations to be closer to the others can reduce this skew, and make the regression model behave a bit better. This can be seen in Figure

13.1011111113.10. On the left, we have raw data, with a single big outlier way to the top-right of the rest of the111113.10.1.1.1.1111111 data. Iʼve estimated OLS twice on this data, once leaving out the outlier and once including it. The estimated OLS slope changes wildly!

![image 84](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile84.png)

Figure 13.10: OLS Fit with and without an Outlier, with and without Log Transformations X Y

On the right, Iʼve taken the logarithm of both and . You can immediately see that the outlier comes in closer to the rest of the

data (this shows up on the graph as the other data spreading out, since we can zoom in more closely on it without losing the outlier). The distance between observations is shrunk at the high end. Also, including or excluding the outlier has a much more muted effect on what the regression line looks like.

And keep in mind, itʼs fine for the presence of the outlier to affect the slope of the OLS line. In fact, it should. Alobservations should have some sort of effect on the regression line. The problem with the graph on the left is that it hastoo muchinfluence for just being a single observation, and we can ease that a little bit with the logarithms.

53

THE SECOND REASON TO TRANSFORM A VARIABLEis to try to get a linear relationship between the variables. Remember, OLS naturally fits a straight line. It can only fit curvy lines if you transform the variables, either by using a polynomial or some other transformation.

This comes down to what kind of relationship you think youʼre modeling. Should a one-unit increase in relate to a -unit increase in ? Then youʼre talking about a linear relationship and you donʼt need a transformation. But what if a one-unit increase in should relate to some

X β1 Y

###### X

percentageincrease in ? Or if a certainpercentageincrease in should relate to a certain increase in ? Or something like that?

###### Y X Y

For example, if we were regressing your current wealth on the interest rate of your investments as of ten years ago, the true relationship is . The effect of an increase in should bemultiplicative, not linear. So we need a transformation to “linearize” the relationship. In this case, a logarithm comes in handy again. If we take the logarithm of both sides, we get

Wealth = InitialWealth × e10×InterestRate InterestRate

ln(Wealth) = ln(InitialWealth) + 10 × InterestRate (13.11)

54

and thatʼs a linear relationship. OLS will have an easy time with that one. The logarithm is handy for turning multiplicative relationships like this into additive linear ones, since

.

log(XY ) = log(X) + log(Y )

So if you have a theoretical relationship, you want to think abouthow those two variables relate. Shouldthe relationship be linear? Or will you need some sort of transformation to get a linear relationship out of it?

WHAT KINDS OF TRANSFORMATIONS ARE THERE, THEN?We have our reasons for transforming now, but what kinds of transformations can we do?

There are a lot of options here and I donʼt plan to go through all of them. But a few tend to pop up regularly in social science research.

The first is, unsurprisingly, the logarithm. Weʼve talked about this one.

The second, third, and fourth come from the problems we have with the logarithm. In particular, the logarithm canʼt handle values of zero; is undefined. So what if we want the nice skewness-handling properties of the logarithm but also have data with zeroes in it?

log(0)

There are three popular options. Our second transformation, after the logarithm, is to simply add one to the variable before taking the logarithm, . This will do the job but also is very

log(x + 1)

much a kludge that tends to lose some of the nice things we liked about the logarithm in the first place. Without going too deep in the weeds, I donʼt recommend it.

The third transformation is thesquare root. Like the logarithm, taking the square root of a variable “squishes down” its big positive outliers. But it can handle a zero, with . However, while using a square root does tamp down on big observations, it doesnʼt do so nearly asmuch as a logarithm does. For example, going from 4 to 10,000, the square root is multiplied by 50 (

√0 = 0

), whereas the logarithm is only multiplied by about 6.6 (

√10000/√4 = 100/2 = 50 log(10000)/log(4) ≈ 9.2/1.4 ≈ 6.6

). Also, you lose the nice “percent increase” interpretation that logarithms have, which weʼll talk about in a bit.

The fourth is theinverse hyperbolic sinetransformation, or “asinh,” which is equal to

. This transformation has the nice property of accepting ( ) as well

ln(x + √x2 + 1) 0 asinh(0) = 0

55

- as negative numbers. Plus, for reasonably large values (say, above 10) it provides results that areverysimilar to a plain-olʼ logarithm. So the nice stuff we like about logarithms we also get with the inverse hyperbolic sine. For skewed data with zeroes where your only goal is to reduce the effect of the skew, the inverse hyperbolic sine is usually what I recommend.


However, if the goal is to get some sort of theoretical percentage-increase interpretation out of the variable, then any sort of ad-hoc method to deal with zeroes (which by nature donʼt really belong with percentage increases) is going to get some strange results. There are some ways to lessen the problem, like ensuring the mean of the variable is large by scaling it up (using dollars rather than thousands of dollars, for example) (⊕Bellemare and Wichman 2020). You may also just be better off with a method that naturally deals with zeroes rather than either or

###### asinh(x) ln(1 + x)

, like Poisson regression or a “two-part” model that treats “is this zero or above-zero” and “what above-zero value is this” as two separate questions (⊕Mullahy and Norton 2024). THE FIFTH TRANSFORMATION WEʼLL DISCUSSdoesnʼt really deal with skew or linearizing a relationship, but it does deal with outliers.Winsorizing, especially popular in finance, is the process of taking some data and very forcefully squishing in its ends. You simply take any values that are far enough away from the center and reduce them toward the center. To Winsorize the top X%, you take every observation above the Xth percentile, and replace it with the Xth percentile.

For example, if we had 100 observations from 1 to 100, and we Winsorized the top and bottom 5%, that would mean leaving the 6-95 observations as they were, and then taking the 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 and replacing them with 6, and also taking the 96, 97, 98, 99, and 100 observations and replacing them with 95.

This is a very brute-force way of dealing with outliers, but it tends to work. It leaves most of the data untouched, which is nice if you think the true relationship really is linear, but you donʼt want your model to be too influenced by outliers.

THE SIXTH AND FINAL TRANSFORMATIONweʼll consider doesnʼt really improve the statistical properties of the model at all. It doesnʼt account for skew, outliers, or linearization. This is the transformation ofstandardizinga variable by subtracting its mean and dividing by its standard deviation, or .

(X − mean(X))/sd(X) 56

Why do this if it doesnʼt improve the statistical properties of the model at all? Because it can make the model easier to interpret. We know that in the model , the interpretation of relies on a “one-unit increase in .” But that means we need to keep in mind the scale of all of our variables so we know how big a one-unit increase is. But if we standardize first, then instead has an interpretation based on a “one-standard-deviation increase in ,” which can be easier to think about in some cases.

Y = β0 + β1X + ε β1 X

β1 X

Same goes for standardizing . Imagine is student test scores, and is time spent watching TV per day. If , then a one-unit increase in TV hours per day reduces test scores by 5. Is that a big effect? A small effect? Weʼd need to know way more about the test itself. But if we standardize

Y Y X β^1 = −5

first, and , then an extra hour of TV a day reduces test scores by half a standard deviation - pretty big! We can more immediately interpret the result now. Naturally, this only works for variables where we have a sense of the standard deviation more than we have a sense of the units.

- Y β^1 = −.5


ONE THING ALL OF THOSE TRANSFORMATIONShave in common is that they arecontinuous transformations. A tiny change in the original variable will translate into a slightly different tiny change in the transformed version. However, we might expect the relevant transformation for a variable to bediscontinuous. For example, letʼs say weʼre interested in the effect of your age on

your chances of driving drunk. In the United States, the legal drinking age is 21. So the relevant way to think about the impact of age on driving drunk might not be “what is the impact of a oneyear increase in age,” but rather “what is the impact of going from below 21 to above 21?” Thereʼs not a smooth change from 20-and-11-months to 21-and-0-months, but rather something sharply shifts.

How can we put this into practice? Well, based on what we think the relevant points at which things shift sharply, we might create a set of categorical indicators. If we want to be fully flexible here, we could have a binary indicator for every possible value of age (this is called “saturating” the model). This lets the relationship between drunk driving and age take literally any shape, letting discontinuities show themselves. On the other hand, there probably arenʼt a lot of people in our data who are exactly, say, 22 years and three months old, so our estimates wonʼt be very precise.

57

We can get a little more power by deciding ahead of time what we think the relevant discontinuities are. We might say “turning 21 is the relevant discontinuity, so Iʼll create a categorical indicator for being below or above 21, and thatʼs how age will be included in my model.” Or we could combine this binary indicator with an interaction term (see later in the chapter) and say “age has a linear effect on drunk driving, but also there is a discontinuous jump

- at 21.” If there are special meaningful values of your variables, then modeling them directly can really help represent the data accurately!


Thinking carefully about discontinuities in your variables is important, and if you know thereʼs a discontinuity but you donʼt account for it, you can harm your model. Take for instancebunching. Bunching is when, even though your variable is continuous, thereʼs a particular value thatʼs very common. Usually this is a “corner value.” For example, how many hours per week do you work at your job? Could reasonably be 12, 13, 42, 16.25, and so on. But youʼre likely to find a lot of people in a given data set who work exactly 40 hours (or however many hours counts as full-time in your country), and alotof people who donʼt have jobs and will work exactly 0 hours.

This bunching can hide back doors! Thereʼs something fundamentally different, probably in a discontinuous and unobservable way, between people who work 0 hours and people who work 1 hour, a difference that you wonʼt get between people who work 1 hour and people who work 2

hours. So the presence of a whole bunch of people in your data who work exactly 0 hours might imply the existence of some back doors, back doors that might be open!

58

In the bunching case, thereʼs a fairly simple transformation you can apply to test whether you should really be concerned about these open back doors. Just take whatever transformation you were already using, and add a binary indicator for being exactly at the bunching point (so an indicator for “works exactly zero hours” in this case). If that coefficient is not close to zero (if you can reject 0), then that bunching is probably leaving some back doors open (⊕Caetano et al. 2021).

AND THATʼS IT- those are the only transformations weʼll cover. There are many, many more

transformations in the world, but in my experience these are the most common in social science research. Honestly, Iʼm not sure Iʼve ever had occasion to use a transformation other than one of these seven, or a polynomial.

That said, just having listed these transformations doesnʼt mean weʼre quite done with them yet. In particular, logarithms - which I just canʼt seem to stop going on about - can make our results a bit trickier to interpret. So how can we do that?

INTERPRETING THE RESULTS OF A REGRESSION WITH A LOGARITHM TRANSFORMATIONappears at first to be no problem. Then you think a little about it, and it seems like a huge problem. Then you learn even more about it, and it once again becomes no problem at all. Such is life.

59

Letʼs look first at a regression with a log transformation on (weʼll get to later).

###### X Y

Y = β0 + β1 ln(X) + ε (13.12)

So how does it appear at first? No problem! We can interpret the as “a one-unit change in is associated with a -unit change in .” Great.

β^1 ln(X) β^1 Y

Now letʼs think a little about it - what exactlyisa one-unit change in ? What does that even mean? Itʼs not the same thing as a one-unit change in .

ln(X) X

Letʼs learn even more about it, at which point it will become easy again. Linear changes in are related toproportionalchanges in . Think about it. We know that . So if we start with and weadd 1 to it, we get . Addingsomething to is equivalent tomultiplyingsomething by .

ln(X)

X ln(AB) = ln(A) + ln(B) ln(X) 1 + ln(X) = ln(e) + ln(X) = ln(eX) 60

ln(X) X

In particular, whatever we add to , letʼs call the thing we add , we are in effect multiplying by . Now we can use a convenient little fact: If that is close to 0, then is close to . Why is that convenient? Because it means that a increase in is approximately equivalent to multiplying by 1.05, or in other words a increase in is approximately equivalent to a5% increase in . Now you know why I was talking earlier about relationships that were proportional in nature.

ln(X) c

- X ec c ec 1 + c


.05 ln(X) X .05 ln(X)

X 61 62

Letʼs take this intuition and apply it so we can interpret a log no matter where it shows up in our equation.

First, back to our original equation with a log transformation for : . Letʼs use a 10% change rather than a 100% change here for , so weʼre more in the realm of reality (for most social science studies, 10% changes show up a lot more often than 100% changes) and so the approximation works better. A 10% increase in translates to a -unit increase in , which is associated with a -unit change in . So we can interpret by saying that a 10% increase in is associated with a -unit change in .

X Y = β0 + β1 ln(X) + ε X

1 + c ≈ ec X 10/100 = .1

- ln(X) .1 × β1 Y β1

- X .1 × β1 Y
- Y ln(Y ) = β0 + β1X + ε X


β1 ln(Y ) β1 × 100 Y β1

ln(Y ) = β0 + β1 ln(X) + ε X 10/100 = .1 ln(X) .1 × β1

- ln(Y ) .1 × β1 × 100 10 × β1 Y 63


Next, letʼs try putting the log on instead: . Now, a 1-unit change in is associated with a -unit change in , which then means a change in (if is small enough that we can use our approximation).

Finally, we can put the log on both. . Now, a 10% increase in is associated with a -unit increase in , which is associated with a -unit change in , which translates into a , or change in .

And thatʼs it! You can memorize those three interpretations if you want, but itʼs probably a better idea to work through the logic each time instead so you know whatʼs going on. Plus, as a bonus, this kind of thinking works foranytransformation. Not all transformations have percentage

interpretations, but you can always think through the logic of “what does a one-unit change in this transformed variablemeanin terms of the original variable?” and work out your interpretation from there.

###### 13.2.4 Interaction Terms

POLYNOMIALS AND TRANSFORMATIONS ARE GREAT FOR LETTINGthe relationship between and be

Y X

flexible. We can fit any sort of curvy line we want, so that the relationship between and can change depending on the value of . But weʼre still missing something. What if the relationship between and differs not based on the value of ,but based on the value of a different

Y X X

- Y X X
- Z


###### variable ?

For example, whatʼs the relationship between the price of gas and how much an individual chooses to drive? For people who own a car, that relationship might be quite strong and negative. For people who donʼt own a car, that relationship is probably near zero. For people who own a car but mostly get around by bike, that relationship might be quite weak. The relationship between gas prices and miles drivendiffers depending on car ownership.We might want to allow the relationship to vary so we can model it properly. We might also be interested in whether the relationshipisdifferent between these groups.

64

However, we have no way of representing this change in relationship with either a polynomial or a transformation. Instead we will need to useinteraction terms.

An interaction term is when you multiply together two different variables and include their product in the regression, like .

Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + β3XZ + ε

In terms of putting the model together, thereʼs not a lot more to it than that. If you think that the relationship between and is different for different values of , then simply include in your model, as well as by itself.

- Y X Z X × Z
- Z 65


The real difficulty with interaction terms isinterpretingthem. Interpretation can be a bit of a brainbender, which is unfortunate; you canʼt walk two steps in a standard social science research design without running into a study where the interaction term is the most important part.

###### SO HOW CAN WE INTERPRET INTERACTION TERMS?The question of interpreting a model with interaction terms is actually two questions. The first iswhat is the effect of a variable when thereʼs an interaction between and something else in the model?The second is ishow can I interpret the interaction term?

###### X X

The intuition for both comes back to good olʼ calculus. If you donʼt know calculus, you should still be able to follow along with the intuition. But if you do know calculus, hey, calculus is pretty neat right? Letʼs be in a calculus club.

66

Letʼs tackle the first question: what is the effect of when thereʼs an interaction between and something else in the model? To solve this, we can write out the regression equation, and then just take the derivative with respect to . Done! After all, the derivative of with respect to is just how changes as changes, which is also the interpretation of a regression coefficient. If you donʼt know calculus, this is a very easy derivative. As long as there arenʼt any polynomial or transformation terms for , just gather all the terms that have an in them, and then remove the . Done. If therearepolynomial or transformation terms for , well… there are calculators online that can do derivatives for you.

X X

X Y X Y X

X X X X

So if our regression is

Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + β3XZ + ε (13.13)

then we have

∂Y ∂X

= β1 + β3Z (13.14)

X Y 67

and thatʼs the effect of on .

Of course, weʼre not entirely done yet, as the effect of that weʼve calculated has a in it. This means that there is no single effect of . Rather, we need to ask what the effect of isat a given value of . We can say what the effect of is at (itʼs just ), or at (itʼs

X Z

X 68 X Z X Z = 0 β1 Z = 16

), or any other value.

β1 + 16β3

This also means that the individual coefficients themselves donʼt tell us much, just like with polynomials. no longer gives us “the effect of .” So if youʼre looking at effect sizes, you need to consider the entire effect-of- expression. Just looking at alone tells you very little (and, further, thesignificanceof tells you very little).

β1 X

X β1 β1

So thatʼs how we can get the effect of when itʼs involved in an interaction term. How about our other question? What is the interpretation of the interaction term itself? Formally, the same approach of taking the derivative works too - in the regression equation

###### X

, the interaction term is the cross-derivative of with respect to both and , i.e., . Thatʼs a little harder to wrap our heads around, though, so we may want to back out and think of it in a more intuitive, less mathematical way.

- Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + β3XZ β3 Y

X Z ∂

2Y ∂X∂Z

- Y = β0 + β1Z Z Y Z X


One good way to think about interpreting interaction terms is this: in the regression

###### , the coefficient on tells ushow our prediction of changes when increases by one unit, right? But as weʼve already established, we can getthe effect of in the equation

by taking the derivative (or gathering the terms and dropping

Y = β0 + β1X + β2Z + β3XZ X

) to get . This looks familiar, doesnʼt it? Now, the coefficient on inthis equation, , tells us how our prediction of changes when increases by one unit. In other words, ishow much stronger the effect of on getswhen increases by one unit.

- X ∂X∂Y = β1 + β3Z Z β3 ∂X∂Y Z

β3 X Y Z 69

- Y = β0 + β1X + β2Child + β3X × Child Child X


Often, the variable being interacted with is binary. This doesnʼt change the interpretation, but it makes it a bit easier to work through. Say we have the regression equation

, where is a binary variable equal to 1 for children and 0 otherwise. What do we have from this equation? We can say that the effect of on

is , just as before, and that is how much stronger the effect of on gets from a one-unit increase in .

Y β1 + β3Child β3 X Y Child 70

But can only increase from 0 to 1 - those are the only values it takes. So if the effect of on

Child X Y β1 + β3Child β1 X Y Child = 0 X Y β1 Child = 0 β1 + β30 = β1 X Y Child = 1 β1 + β31 = β1 + β3

is , then that means that is the effect of on when , i.e., the effect of on for non-children is . We get that by just taking and plugging it in to get . To get the effect of on for children, we plug in and get

.

The difference between those two effects - for children and for non-children, is . This is the difference in the effect of on between children and non-children. Or, put another way, itʼs how much stronger the effect of on gets when you increase by 1, going from

β1 + β3 β1 β3 X Y

X Y Child Child = 0 Child = 1

to , i.e., non-child to child. Thatʼs the same interpretation we had before. If we find a meaningful (or, if you prefer, statistically significant) coefficient on , then we can say that “the effect of on differs between children and non-children.”

β3 X Y

NumberofLocations NumberofLocations

NumberofLocations InspectionScore Y earofInspection = 0

71 Y earofInspection = 2008

in 2008 is . Positive! In fact, the effect is positive for the entire range of years actually in the data, despite that big negative -1.466. Donʼt be tricked by the coefficients on their own. Always interpret them with the relevant interactions. We can also say that every year, the relationship between a one-unit increase in and gets more positive by .001.

NumberofLocations InspectionScore

In the third column, we have an effect of of . From this, we can say that on a non-weekend, the association between a one-unit increase in

NumberofLocations −.019 − .010Weekend

and is , and on the weekend, itʼs

NumberofLocations InspectionScore −.019 −019 − .010 = −.029 72

. We can also say that the relationship is .010 more negative on weekends than it is on weekdays.

73

THERE ARE A FEW THINGS TO KEEP IN MINDwhen it comes to using interaction terms. The first is to think very carefully aboutwhyyou are including a given interaction term, because they are prone to fluke results if you go fishing. Think way back to Chapter11 - if we just let the data do whatever,1 weʼll find results that donʼt mean much. We need to approach research and data with a specific design. Have a strong theoretical reasonwhyyouʼd expect the effect of to vary across different values of before adding the interaction term. “I dunno, maybe the effect is different” isnʼt a fantastic reason.

###### X Z X × Z

Itʼs always a good idea to approach data with a design and an intention rather than just fishing around, interaction terms or not. Why do I emphasize this so strongly when it comes to interaction terms? First, because there are alotof different interactions youcould try, and a lot of

them seem tantalizing. Does the effect of job training differ between genders? Between races? Between different ages? Sure, it might. We can tell ourselves a good story about why each of those might be great things to interact with job training. But if we tryalof them, weʼre likely to find a significant interaction pretty quickly even if thereʼs really nothing there. So trying interactions all willy-nilly tends to lead to false positives.

The temptation to try a bunch of stuff is extra-hard to resist when you have a null effect. Sure, maybe we didnʼt find any effect of job training. But maybe the effect is only there for women! Try a gender interaction and look for to be big/significant even when isnʼt. Or maybe itʼs only there for Pacific Islanders! Try a race interaction. Or maybe only in Iowa. Try a state interaction. However, because there are nearly infinite different sub-groups wecouldlook at (especially once you consider going deeper - maybe the effect is only there for women Pacific Islanders in Iowa!), weʼre almost certain to find some effect if we check every single one. In general, you should be highly skeptical of an effect thatisnʼt there at alfor the whole sample, but isthere for some sub-group. Surely there are plenty of effects that are, in truth, only there for a certain sub-group. But fishing around with a bunch of interactions is going to give you way too many false positives. So stick to cases where thereʼs a strong theoretical reasonwhyyouʼd expect the effect to only be there for a sub-group, or differ across groups, and make sure you believe your story before you check the data.

###### β1 + β3 β1

Second, even if we do have a strong idea of a difference in effect that might be there, interaction terms arenoisy. Theyʼre basically looking for thedifference betweentwo noisy things - the effect for one group and the effect for the other group, both of which have sampling variation. The difference between two noisy things will be noisier still. You need alotmore observations to precisely estimate an interaction term than to precisely estimate a full-sample estimate. Even if the difference is as large as one group having an effect twice the size of the other group, you may need sixteen times as many observations to have adequate statistical power for the interaction term as youʼd need for the main term (⊕Gelman 2018).

74 75

Why is noisiness a problem? Because estimates that are noisy will more often get surprisingly big effects. The sampling variation is very wide with noisy estimates. So thereʼs a better chance that when youdofind an interaction term big enough to call it interesting, itʼs just sampling variation, even if the result is statistically significant!

That said, interaction terms are still very much worthwhile if you have a solid reason to include

- them. And theyʼre super important to learn for causal inference. In fact, many of the research designs in this second half of the book are based around interaction terms. Turns out you can get a lot of identification mileage out of looking at how the effect of your treatment variable differs between, say, conditions where it should have a causal effect and conditions where any effect it has is just back-door paths. So give yourself some time to practice interpreting and using them. It will definitely pay off.


###### 13.2.5 Coding Up Polynomials and Interactions

In these two coding blocks weʼre going to be continuing to work with the restaurant-inspection data weʼve been covering in this chapter.

First, weʼll do a regression with a polynomial in it. Then, weʼll calculate the effect of

for a given value of the variable in such a way that also gives us a standard error on the effect at that value.

NumberofLocations

###### R Code R Code e Stata CodeStata CodeStatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatata e Python CodePython Codeytyttt ooooooooooo o o oooooo o o o e

# Load packages and data library(tidyverse); library(modelsummary); library(marginaleffects) df <- causaldata::restaurant_inspections

# Use I() to add calculations of variables # Including squares m1 <- lm(inspection_score ~ NumberofLocations +

I(NumberofLocations^2) + Year, data = df)

# Print the results to a table msummary(m1, stars = c('*' = .1, '**' = .05, '***' = .01))

# Use avg_slopes to calculate the effect of locations # at 100 locations. # variables is the variable we want the effect of # and newdata can be used with datagrid to get the effect # at a particular value avg_slopes(m1,

variables = 'NumberofLocations', newdata = datagrid(NumberofLocations = 100))

# The Estimate is the AME (average marginal effect), which # we want. The standard error is reported, too.

Next, we will include an interaction term. Similarly, we will calculate the effect of locations at a specific value of the interacting variable (remember, the interaction term itself already shows the differencebetween effects, we donʼt need that from a separate command).

###### R Code R Code e Stata CodeStata CodeStatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatata e Python CodePython Codeytyttt ooooooooooo o o oooooo o o o e

# Load packages library(tidyverse); library(modelsummary); library(marginaleffects) df <- causaldata::restaurant_inspections

# Use * to include two variables independently # plus their interaction # (: is interaction-only, we rarely use it) m1 <- lm(inspection_score ~ NumberofLocations*Weekend +

Year, data = df)

# Print the results to a table msummary(m1, stars = c('*' = .1, '**' = .05, '***' = .01))

# Use avg_slopes to calculate the effect of locations # on the weekend # variables is the variable we want the effect of # and newdata can be used with datagrid to get the effect # at a particular value avg_slopes(m1,

variables = 'NumberofLocations', newdata = datagrid(Weekend = TRUE))

# The Estimate is the AME (average marginal effect), which # we want. The standard error is reported, too.

###### 13.2.6 Nonlinear Regression

SO FAR, WHILE WEʼVE ALLOWED THE PREDICTOR VARIABLES TO BE ALL KINDS OF THINGS- binary, skewed, related in nonlinear ways to the dependent variable - weʼve kept a tighter leash on the outcome variable. Weʼve always assumed that we can predict the conditional mean of the dependent variable using a linear function of the predictors. In other words, we can predict the dependent variable by taking our predictors, multiplying them each by their coefficients, and adding everything up.

76

However, that wonʼt always work. To be able to predict something with a linear function, it needs to behave like, well, a line. It needs to change smoothly as the predictors change (so it canʼt, for

example, be a discrete value that jumps up rather than climbs up). Also, it needs to continue off forever in either direction, i.e., take any value - if your estimated model is, for example

, then your prediction for someone with $50,000 in is -.5 children. That doesnʼt make much sense.

###### NumberofChildren = 4.5 − .1IncomeinThousands Income

That brings us tononlinear regression. This is a big wide world of approaches to performing regression that donʼt rely on a linear relationship between the predictors and the outcome variable. These approaches are usually tailored to the exact kind of dependent variable youʼre dealing with. So in this chapter (unfortunately), all weʼre going to be able to cover are the probit and logit models for dealing with binary dependent variables. But thereʼs also Poisson regression for count variables, multinomial logit and probit for categorical data, tobit for censored data, and so on and so on.

77

Even though using linear regression for these strange dependent variables doesnʼt make sense, some people do it anyway. There are actually some good reasons to do OLS anyway even when the dependent variable isnʼt quite right. All of these nonlinear regression models add their own assumptions, and generally donʼt have closed-form solutions, making their estimates a little less stable. They can have a hard time with things like controls for categorical variable with lots of categories. Plus, while using OLS with a binary outcome guarantees your error terms are heteroskedastic (weʼll talk about this in a few sections), the presence of heteroskedasticity actually makes thecoefficients wrongin logit and probit models, and a specialized heteroskedasticity-robust version of the estimators is required. In the context of binary dependent variables, using OLS anyway is called the “linear probability model,” and it does see a fair bit of use.

78

That said, while in some cases the nonlinear-regression cure is worse than the incorrect-linearmodel disease, generally it is not, and you want to use the appropriate model. Why exactly is that? As I mentioned earlier, Iʼll be going through why we do this, and how it works, specifically in application to binary dependent variables.

GENERALIZED LINEAR MODELS (GLMS) ARE A WAY OF EXTENDING WHAT WE ALREADY KNOW ABOUT REGRESSION to a broader context where we need some nonlinearity. GLMs, arenʼt the only way that

nonlinear regression is done, but itʼs how probit and logit are often done, and it applies in some other contexts too. And itʼs pretty easy to think about once weʼve covered regression.

GLM says this: take that regression model, and pass it through a function in order to make your prediction. We call this function a “link function,” and we call the input to that function the “index.” Thatʼs it! So our regression equation is

###### 79 F()

Y = F(β0 + β1X) (13.15)

Notice that the index inside the function is the exact same regression equation we were working with before. Also notice that if we make be just , then weʼre back to again and are basically doing OLS.

β0 + β1X F()

80 F(x) F(x) = x Y = β0 + β1X

How does GLM let us run nonlinear regression? As long as we pick a function that mimics the properties that has, then we can ensure that we never make an unrealistic prediction.

###### Y

In the case of binary variables, we are predicting the probability that is equal to 1. In other words, should give us a probability. Probabilities should be between 0 and 1. So what are we looking for in a link function that works with a binary ?

Y F(β0 + β1X)

###### Y

It should be able to take any value from to (since can take any value from to ) It should only produce values between 0 and 1

−∞ ∞ β0 + β1X −∞ ∞

Whenever the input ( ) increases, the output should increase

β0 + β1X

There are infinitely many functions that satisfy these criteria, but two are most popular: thelogit linkfunction and theprobit linkfunction.

The logit link function is

ex 1 + ex

F(x) =

(13.16)

and the probit link function is , where is the “cumulative distribution function” of the standard normal distribution, i.e., tells you the percentile of the standard normal distribution (mean 0, standard deviation 1) that is.

Φ(x) Φ Φ(x)

x

These two link functions - probit and logit - produce nearly identical regression predictions. You donʼt generally need to worry too hard about which of the two you use. The important thing, though, is that neither of them will make predictions outside of the range of 0 and 1, and their coefficients will be estimated in a context that is aware of those boundaries and so better-suited for those non-continuous dependent variables.

81

WHATʼS SO BAD ABOUT PEACE, LOVE, AND LINEARITY?Why not just stick with OLS? What weʼre really interested in is how increases in cause changes in . Surely getting identification sorted is the real important step, and we donʼt necessarily care what values of arepredictedas long as we still get those effects, right?

X Y

Y

Two problems with this argument. First,anytime youʼre estimating a model thatʼs not the right model, strange results can creep in even in unexpected ways, and in ways that can be hard to predict ahead of time. Second, not properly accounting for nonlinearitycanmess up your estimates of how affects .

82

X Y

We can see this in action in Figure13.1111111113.11. Here we have a bunch of observations that are either111113.11.11.11.11.11111111111111

or at different values of . On top of that graph we have the fitted OLS model as well as a fitted logit model (the probit would look very similar).

Y = 0 Y = 1 X

Figure 13.11: OLS and Logit Fit on Binary Dependent Variable

![image 85](huntington-klein-2025-the-effect-textbook_images/imageFile85.png)

What do we see? As previously mentioned, we see the OLS prediction going outside the range of 0 and 1. But beyond that,the slopes of the OLS and logit models are different.This is especially true near the right or left sides of the graph. What does this mean? It means that the reason we were thinking about using OLS anyway - getting the effect of on rather than caring about realistic predictions - falls apart anyway.

X Y

But why is this? Well, think about it - if has a positive effect on , but you already predict that

X Y

has a .999 chance of occurring, then what should a one-unit increase in do? Almost nothing, right? Thereʼs simply not that much more that can increase by. But if your current prediction of

- Y X Y


is .5, then thereʼs a lot more room to grow, so the effect can be bigger. Itshouldbe bigger! OLS is unable to account for this.

Y

83

OLS is unable to account for how the effect of on should differ. This works in a different way to how the effect differed when we talked about polynomials. The curviness shouldnʼt be based onthe value of , as it is with a polynomial, it should be based onthe current predicted value of

X Y

X

.When the sample average of is in the middle - near .5 or so - using OLS isnʼt a huge problem, although you still might want to use probit or logit anyway. But if the average of is anywhere near 0 or 1, you have a big problem and absolutely need a nonlinear model.

###### Y YY

INTERPRETING A GENERALIZED LINEAR MODELcan be a little trickier than interpreting an OLS model. In one, very narrow and pretty useless sense, the interpretations are exactly the same. That is to say, each coefficient still means “a one-unit change in this variable is associated with a (coefficient-sized) change in the index.” Thatʼs true whether weʼre talking about linear or nonlinear regression. The difference, however, is that with OLS, the index function is just a regression equation giving us the conditional mean of the dependent variable, and we know how to interpret a change in the dependent variable. In GLM, the index function is the thing that gets put through the link function. So itʼs in terms of “units of change in the thing that gets put through the link function,” which is way harder to think about.

84

Itʼs common to present the results of probit and logit models, and nonlinear regression more generally, in terms of theirmarginal effects. Marginal effects basically translate things back into linear-model terms. While the coefficient on in a logit model gives the effect of a change in on the index, the marginal effect of gives the effect of a change in on the probability that the dependent variable is 1.

###### X X X X

Mathematically, the marginal effect is straightforward, although we do have to dip back into the calculus well once again. The marginal effect of on the probability that is just the derivative of with respect to , which has a conveniently simple solution for these binary-dependent variable models:

X Y = 1 Pr(Y = 1) X

∂Pr(Y = 1) ∂X

= β1Pr(Y = 1)(1 − Pr(Y = 1)) (13.17)

So the marginal effect of is equal to the coefficient on , , multiplied by the predicted probability that (which comes from the model, and is conditional on all the predictor values) and one minus that same probability. Note that is tiny when

X X β1 Pr(Y = 1)

Pr(Y = 1)(1 − Pr(Y = 1)) Pr(Y = 1) Pr(Y = 1) = .5

is near 0 or 1, and at its biggest when , so we retain the result from Figure13.1111111113.11 that the slope is steepest in the “middle” of the graph and flattest on the sides.111113.11.11.11.11.11111111111111

There is, however, a catch. That catch is that there is no one marginal effect for a given variable. I just said to look back at Figure13.1111111113.11. Well… keep looking at it! As111113.11.11.11.11.11111111111111 increases, the logitpredicted probability that might go up by a tiny amount (on the left or right side of the graph) or by quite a bit (in the middle). In fact, each observation in the data has its own marginal effect, based on the for that observation, as weʼd expect based on the

###### X Y = 1

Pr(Y = 1) β1Pr(Y = 1)(1 − Pr(Y = 1))

equation for the marginal effect. If the model predicts youʼre 90% likely to have , your marginal effect is . If it predicts youʼre 40% likely, your marginal effect is . So what value do we give as “the” marginal effect?

Y = 1 β1(.9)(.1) β1(.4)(.6) 85

There are two common approaches to this issue, one of which I will heavily recommend over the other. The first, which I will not recommend, is the “marginal effect at the mean.” The marginal effect at the mean first takes the mean of all the predictor variables. If weʼre just working with ,

X X Pr(Y = 1)

- then it takes the mean of . Then, it uses that mean to predict . And finally it uses that predicted to calculate the marginal effect, . This gives the marginal effect of an observation with completely average predictors. Why donʼt I recommend this? Because itʼs calculating the marginal effect for no person in particular. Who is this marginal effect for, anyway? Plus, marginal effects at the mean take the mean of each variable independently, when really these variables are likely to be correlated. Itʼs strange to take the mean of everything, for example, if someone with a near-mean value of almost always has a near-minimum value of .


Pr(Y = 1) β1Pr(Y = 1)(1 − Pr(Y = 1))

86

###### X Z

What I recommend instead is theaverage marginal effect. The average marginal effect calculates each individual observationʼs marginal effect, using that observationʼs predictors to get

and then . Then, with each individual observationʼs marginal effect in hand, it takes the average to get the average marginal effect. Thatʼs it! Then you have the mean of the marginal effect across the sample, which gives you an idea of the representative marginal effect.

###### Pr(Y = 1) β1Pr(Y = 1)(1 − Pr(Y = 1))

87

To take a quick example, letʼs say we estimated the logit model

. And for simplicityʼs sake letʼs say we did this with only two observations in the sample. The first observation has and , and the second has and .

Pr(Y = 1) = logit(.1 + .05X − .03Z)

X = 1 Z = 2 X = 0 Z = 20

The predicted value for the first observation is

. Similarly, the predicted value for the second observation is

.99

Pr(Y = 1) = logit(.1 + .05(1) − .03(2)) = logit(.99) = e

1+e.99 ≈ .729

. Then, the marginal effect of

−.5

Pr(Y = 1) = logit(.1 + 0 − .03(20)) = logit(−.5) = e

1+e−.5 ≈ .622 X X .05(.729)(1 − .729) = .010

, since the coefficient on is .05, would be for the first observation, and for the second. Average those out to get the average marginal effect of .

.05(.622)(1 − .622) = .012 (.010 + .012)/2 = .011

LETʼS CODE UP SOME PROBIT AND LOGIT MODELS!In the following code, Iʼll show how to estimate a logit model, and then how to estimate average marginal effects for those models. What about probit? Conveniently, all of the following code samples can be used to estimate probit models, simply by replacing the word “logit” with the word “probit” wherever you see it.

In each case, weʼll be seeing whether weekend inspections have become more or less prevalent over time, once again with our restaurant inspection data.

In the case of the R code, you will often see guides online telling you to use the logitmfx function from the mfx package, instead of marginaleffects. This works fine, but do be aware that it defaults to the marginal effect at the mean instead of the average marginal effect.

###### R Code R Code e Stata CodeStata CodeStatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatatata e Python CodePython Codeytyttt ooooooooooo o o oooooo o o o e

# Load packages and data library(tidyverse); library(modelsummary); library(marginaleffects) df <- causaldata::restaurant_inspections

# Use the glm() function with # the family = binomial(link = 'logit') option m1 <- glm(Weekend ~ Year, data = df,

family = binomial(link = 'logit'))

# See the results msummary(m1, stars = c('*' = .1, '**' = .05, '***' = .01))

# Get the average marginal effect of year avg_slopes(m1, variables = 'Year')

###### 13.3 Your Standard Errors Are Probably Wrong
